text: # Cleaning Deductions ## The Core Principle A landlord can only deduct for cleaning if the property was left in a worse condition than it was received, accounting for fair wear and tear. The landlord cannot charge for cleaning simply because they want professional cleaning done — they must show actual additional cleaning was required. ## Check-In Condition Is Critical The check-in inventory is the baseline for any cleaning dispute. If the property was not professionally cleaned at the start of the tenancy: - The landlord cannot demand professional cleaning at the end - The tenant only needs to return the property to the same standard as received - Any check-in inventory noting dirty oven, limescale, or grease is strong evidence for the tenant ## What Landlords Must Prove To justify a cleaning deduction, the landlord should provide: 1. Evidence that the property was professionally cleaned at check-in (invoice or statement) 2. Evidence of the specific cleaning required at check-out (check-out report with photos) 3. An actual invoice or receipt for the cleaning carried out 4. Evidence that the cleaning cost was reasonable and competitive ## Common Cleaning Disputes ### Oven Cleaning - If the oven had grease marks at check-in, the tenant only needs to return it to that standard - A £100+ oven cleaning charge is often disproportionate for light use marks - Professional oven cleaning is typically £50-80 ### Carpet Cleaning - If carpets were described as "worn" or "stained" at check-in, charges are harder to justify - End-of-tenancy carpet cleaning is not always required - Normal dust and light marks are wear and tear ### General Deep Clean - Charges of £300-500 for a standard flat are often excessive - Tenants should request itemised invoices - Compare the check-in inventory condition with the check-out report ## Key Evidence to Request Tenants should always ask the landlord or agent for: - Check-in cleaning evidence (invoice or inventory statement) - Check-out report with photos - Cleaning invoice with company name, date, and itemised costs - Any comparison photos taken at check-in vs. check-out ## Proportionality Even if cleaning is needed, the charge must be proportionate to actual loss. A landlord cannot charge for a full deep clean of the entire property if only one room required additional cleaning. ## Template Response Points - "Please provide evidence that the property was professionally cleaned at the start of the tenancy." - "Please provide a copy of the cleaning invoice showing itemised costs." - "The check-in inventory notes [condition] which suggests this level of cleaning was pre-existing." - "I dispute the cleaning charge as the property was returned in the same standard as received."

# Deposit Dispute Process ## Step-by-Step Dispute Process ### Step 1: Respond to Deduction Proposal When a landlord proposes deductions: - Do NOT agree to release any disputed amount - Respond in writing (email is fine) within a reasonable time (typically 10 working days) - State clearly which deductions you dispute and why - Ask for supporting evidence for each deduction ### Step 2: Request Evidence For each deduction, request: - Invoices or receipts for work done - Dated photographs (before and after where relevant) - Check-in and check-out inventory/reports - Evidence of what condition the property was in at the start of the tenancy ### Step 3: Negotiate Try to reach a reasonable settlement. If the landlord has some valid evidence: - Consider whether a partial payment is appropriate - Do not agree to an amount higher than reasonably justified - Always get any agreement in writing ### Step 4: Involve the Deposit Scheme If you cannot reach agreement within 10 days of the end of the tenancy: - Contact your deposit protection scheme - Request the disputed amount be protected pending resolution - Apply to the scheme's Alternative Dispute Resolution (ADR) service Both parties must consent to ADR, but it is free and binding. ### Step 5: ADR Submission When submitting to ADR: - Submit all your evidence (check-in inventory, photos, emails, invoices you have received) - Write a clear statement explaining each dispute - Be factual and professional - Reference the check-in condition for each item disputed ### Step 6: Adjudicator Decision An independent adjudicator will: - Review all evidence from both sides - Make a decision based on the evidence - Award amounts based on actual loss and betterment principles The decision is binding on both parties. ## Key Timelines | Event | Deadline | |---|---| | Deposit must be protected | Within 30 days of receipt | | Prescribed Information must be provided | Within 30 days of receipt | | Deposit must be returned (agreed amount) | Within 10 days of agreement | | Landlord must return deposit at end if no dispute raised | Typically 10 days | ## What to Include in Your Dispute Letter A good dispute letter should: 1. Reference the property address and tenancy dates 2. State the total deposit amount 3. List each deduction you dispute with reasons 4. Request specific evidence for each charge 5. State that you do not consent to release of disputed amount 6. Reference relevant laws if applicable (Tenant Fees Act, fair wear and tear) 7. Set a reasonable deadline for response (10-14 days) 8. Be polite and professional in tone ## Evidence Hierarchy (Strongest to Weakest) 1. Signed check-in inventory showing pre-existing condition 2. Dated photographs taken at check-in 3. Landlord's own check-out report showing damage 4. Professional invoices or receipts 5. Emails and written communications 6. Witness statements 7. Unsigned or undated documents ## Small Claims Court If the deposit was not protected, or the landlord refuses to engage with ADR, the tenant can apply to the County Court using the small claims process. Court fees apply, but are recovered if successful.
the tenant can apply to the County Court using the small claims process. Court fees apply, but are recovered if successful.

# Deposit Protection Overview ## Legal Requirement In England and Wales, landlords must protect tenancy deposits in a government-approved scheme within 30 days of receiving the deposit. The three approved schemes are: - **Deposit Protection Service (DPS)** - custodial and insured options - **MyDeposits** - insured scheme - **Tenancy Deposit Scheme (TDS)** - custodial and insured options ## What Must Be Protected Any deposit for an Assured Shorthold Tenancy (AST) that started on or after 6 April 2007 must be protected. This includes: - Cash deposits - Debit/credit card payments used as deposit - Deposits held by letting agents on the landlord's behalf ## Prescribed Information Within 30 days of receiving the deposit, the landlord must also provide the tenant with "Prescribed Information" including: - Name and contact details of the scheme - How to apply to have the deposit returned - What to do if there is a dispute ## Consequences of Non-Compliance If a landlord fails to protect a deposit or provide Prescribed Information, the tenant can apply to court and may receive between 1x and 3x the deposit amount as a penalty, in addition to the return of the deposit itself. An unprotected deposit also prevents the landlord from serving a valid Section 21 notice. ## Checking Deposit Protection Tenants can check if their deposit is protected using the free checking tools on each scheme's website. You will need: - Your tenancy start date - Your deposit amount - Your postcode or the landlord/agent's postcode ## Key Tenant Rights - Tenant is entitled to know which scheme protects the deposit - Tenant must receive Prescribed Information - Deposit can only be deducted for specific reasons set out in the tenancy agreement - Deposit must be returned within 10 days of agreement on the amount ## Dispute Resolution All three schemes offer free Alternative Dispute Resolution (ADR) if landlord and tenant cannot agree. The ADR process is: 1. Tenant and landlord cannot agree on deductions 2. Either party applies to the scheme's ADR service 3. Both parties submit evidence to an independent adjudicator 4. Adjudicator makes a binding decision 5. Deposit is distributed according to the decision The tenant should NOT agree to release any disputed portion while the dispute is ongoing.

# Evidence Checklist for Deposit Disputes ## Tenant's Key Documents ### Essential Documents - [ ] Signed tenancy agreement (including any clauses about cleaning, damage, deposit) - [ ] Deposit protection certificate or Prescribed Information document - [ ] Check-in inventory (signed or unsigned) - [ ] Check-out report (if provided) - [ ] Any emails or messages from landlord or agent about the deposit ### Supporting Evidence - [ ] Photos taken at check-in (with timestamps if possible) - [ ] Photos taken at check-out (with timestamps) - [ ] Copy of any cleaning invoices from end of tenancy - [ ] Receipts for any repairs made before leaving - [ ] Communications about repairs during the tenancy (shows landlord was aware of issues) ## What to Ask the Landlord For For each deduction, ask: ### For Cleaning Charges - Evidence property was professionally cleaned at start of tenancy - Itemised cleaning invoice with company name, date, and specific tasks - Check-in vs. check-out comparison photos - Statement of what specific cleaning was required beyond the check-in standard ### For Damage Claims - Dated photos of the alleged damage - Repair invoice or quote with itemised breakdown - Evidence that the damage was not present at check-in - Justification for cost, especially for replacements (age of item, expected lifespan) ### For Repainting - Photos of the damage requiring repainting - Invoice from decorator - Evidence of how the damage occurred - Explanation of why normal scuffs do not constitute fair wear and tear in this case ### For Replacement Items - Age of item at start of tenancy - Evidence of condition at check-in - Invoice for replacement - Justification for full replacement rather than repair ## Evaluating Evidence Strength ### Strong Tenant Evidence (disputes deduction) - Check-in inventory showing same/similar condition - Landlord's photos that actually show the same or worse condition was present at check-in - No professional cleaning invoice from check-in (counters cleaning charge) - Tenant Fees Act prohibition applies (e.g., mandatory clean clause) - Long tenancy (more fair wear and tear expected) - No signed check-out report from tenant - No dated photos from landlord ### Weak Tenant Position (deduction may be valid) - Check-out report clearly showing damage not in check-in inventory - Dated photos clearly showing new damage - Signed check-out report - Tenant admits to the damage in writing - Professional invoices for work done ## Key Questions to Answer 1. Was the deposit protected in an approved scheme within 30 days? 2. Was Prescribed Information provided? 3. Was there a signed check-in inventory? 4. What condition was the property in at check-in? 5. What specific damage is claimed and is it proven with photos? 6. Are the charges proportionate to actual loss? 7. Does fair wear and tear apply to any of the charges? 8. Are any charges prohibited under the Tenant Fees Act? 9. Has the landlord provided invoices or just estimates? 10. Is the tenancy still within the ADR window (not all schemes have a time limit, but check)?
window (not all schemes have a time limit, but check)?

# Fair Wear and Tear ## Definition Fair wear and tear refers to the normal deterioration of a property and its contents that occurs through ordinary, reasonable use during the tenancy. Landlords cannot charge tenants for fair wear and tear. The longer the tenancy and the more occupants, the greater the expected wear and tear. ## What Counts as Fair Wear and Tear - Small scuffs, marks, and scratches on walls and paintwork - Faded or worn carpets in high-traffic areas (hallways, living rooms, stairs) - Minor carpet indentations from furniture - Small nail holes from hanging pictures - Faded curtains or blinds - Worn light switches and door handles - Light limescale in bathrooms and kitchens - Minor discolouration of grout - Small chips on skirting boards and door frames - Faded paint from sunlight - Worn or faded upholstery from normal use ## What Does NOT Count as Fair Wear and Tear - Burns, stains, or large tears in carpets - Large holes in walls - Broken fixtures and fittings - Damage caused by neglect or misuse - Pet damage (scratches, stains, odours) - Deliberate damage - Broken appliances due to misuse - Significant mould caused by poor ventilation by tenant - Graffiti or unauthorised painting ## Decoration / Repainting Repainting is one of the most commonly disputed charges. Key principles: - Normal scuffs and marks on walls are wear and tear, especially in a long tenancy - A landlord cannot charge for full repainting after a 2-year tenancy simply because paint looks worn - If walls were already scuffed or marked at check-in, no repainting charge should apply - A landlord must show that damage beyond wear and tear occurred - Cost of repainting must be proportionate — you cannot charge for a full repaint if only one wall needs attention ## Age and Depreciation When an item needs replacing, its age must be considered. If a carpet was already 5 years old at the start of a 2-year tenancy, the tenant should only pay for the remaining useful life, not the full replacement cost. The government Betterment Principle: a landlord cannot profit from a deposit claim by replacing an old item with a brand new one at the tenant's full expense. ## Adjudicator Approach Deposit scheme adjudicators apply betterment principles when calculating awards. They typically: - Reduce claims for old or worn items - Reject charges where check-in condition already showed the issue - Require landlords to provide evidence of actual damage beyond wear and tear ## Tenancy Length Guidance (Rough Guide) | Item | Expected Lifespan | Wear and Tear Rate | |---|---|---| | Carpet (mid-range) | 10 years | 10% per year | | Paintwork | 5-7 years | 15-20% per year | | Curtains/blinds | 5 years | 20% per year | | Upholstered furniture | 7-10 years | 10-15% per year | These are guides, not fixed rules, but adjudicators use similar frameworks.

# High-Risk Situations — When to Escalate ## This Tool's Limits Deposit Defender AI is designed to help with standard tenancy deposit disputes. Some situations go beyond a deposit dispute and require professional legal help, specialist housing charities, or urgent action. ## When to Contact Citizens Advice Immediately - You have received a Section 21 (no-fault eviction) notice and are unsure if it is valid - You have received a Section 8 (fault-based eviction) notice - You are being threatened with eviction or asked to leave immediately - Your landlord has entered the property without notice or permission - You are being harassed or intimidated by the landlord or their agents - Your landlord has changed the locks or removed your belongings ## When to Contact Shelter Shelter is a housing charity offering free specialist advice: - You are at risk of homelessness - You have been illegally evicted - You are experiencing harassment from your landlord - You need urgent housing advice - The deposit dispute involves a significant amount of money affecting your housing ## Illegal Eviction Changing locks, removing belongings, switching off utilities, or physically intimidating a tenant to leave is illegal. This is a criminal offence under the Protection from Eviction Act 1977. If you are experiencing illegal eviction: - Call the police if you are in immediate danger - Contact your local council's housing department (they have emergency powers) - Contact Shelter or Citizens Advice ## Unprotected Deposit If your deposit was not protected in an approved scheme within 30 days of receipt, this is a serious legal issue that goes beyond a standard dispute: - The landlord may be liable to pay you 1-3 times the deposit as a penalty - Any Section 21 notice served while the deposit is unprotected is invalid - You may need to apply to the County Court to recover the deposit and penalty This situation requires separate legal advice or assistance from Citizens Advice. ## Court Proceedings If: - The landlord has applied to court for possession - You have received court papers - A court date has been set You need immediate legal advice. Court deadlines are strict. Contact: - Citizens Advice - Your local council housing duty officer - Legal Aid (if eligible) - A solicitor ## Discrimination If you believe you are being treated differently because of a protected characteristic (race, sex, disability, religion, sexual orientation, age, pregnancy, etc.), this may be unlawful under the Equality Act 2010. Contact Citizens Advice or a solicitor. ## Very Large Financial Claims If the total amount in dispute exceeds £1,000-2,000, or represents a significant portion of your financial situation, consider: - Getting independent legal advice - Using Citizens Advice for a case assessment - Considering small claims court if ADR is not available or fails ## Key Helplines - **Citizens Advice**: 0800 144 8848 (England) | 0800 702 2020 (Wales) - **Shelter**: 0808 800 4444 (England) | 0808 800 4444 (Scotland) - **Shelter Webchat**: shelter.org.uk - **Gov.uk deposit schemes**: gov.uk/tenancy-deposit-protection ## Deposit Defender AI Disclaimer This tool helps tenants organise evidence and prepare dispute communications. It does not: - Provide legal advice - Replace a solicitor, Citizens Advice, Shelter, or your deposit protection scheme - Guarantee any outcome - Represent you in any legal or ADR proceeding Always verify guidance with a qualified professional before taking legal action.

It does not: - Provide legal advice - Replace a solicitor, Citizens Advice, Shelter, or your deposit protection scheme - Guarantee any outcome - Represent you in any legal or ADR proceeding Always verify guidance with a qualified professional before taking legal action.

# Tenant Fees Act 2019 ## Overview The Tenant Fees Act 2019 came into force on 1 June 2019. It bans most fees that landlords and letting agents can charge tenants in England. Similar legislation applies in Wales (Renting Homes (Fees etc.) (Wales) Act 2019) and Scotland (Tenant Fees Act — Scotland has had a ban since 2012). ## Permitted Payments Only Landlords and agents can only charge tenants for: 1. **Rent** — the agreed monthly rent 2. **Deposit** — capped at 5 weeks' rent (or 6 weeks if annual rent exceeds £50,000) 3. **Holding deposit** — capped at 1 week's rent, refundable in most cases 4. **Changes to tenancy** — capped at £50 (or reasonable costs if higher) for tenant-requested changes like adding a permitted occupier 5. **Early termination** — only if the tenant requests it, and costs must not exceed landlord's losses 6. **Default fees** — only if explicitly stated in the tenancy agreement: - **Lost keys** — reasonable costs for replacement - **Late rent** — only after rent is 14 days overdue, capped at 3% above Bank of England base rate ## What Is Banned The following fees are prohibited: - Admin fees for setting up or renewing a tenancy - Inventory fees - Check-in and check-out fees - Cleaning fees as a condition of the tenancy (i.e. requiring professional clean at end) - Reference check fees - Credit check fees - Professional cleaning clauses (mandatory end-of-tenancy cleaning) - Gardening fees not in the agreement - General administration charges - Fee for renewing a tenancy ## Mandatory Cleaning Clauses A clause requiring the tenant to pay for professional cleaning at the end of the tenancy regardless of condition is now a prohibited payment under the Tenant Fees Act 2019. Such clauses cannot be enforced. However, if the property is left dirtier than it was received, the landlord can still claim for the actual cost of cleaning required — this is about actual damage/loss, not a mandatory fee. ## Consequences of Charging Prohibited Fees - Any prohibited payment must be returned to the tenant within 28 days - Trading Standards can impose a fine of up to £5,000 for a first offence - Subsequent offences can result in a fine of up to £30,000 or a banning order - A landlord who has charged a prohibited payment cannot serve a valid Section 21 notice until the payment has been returned ## Deposit Cap Since 1 June 2019, deposits are capped at: - **5 weeks' rent** for properties with annual rent under £50,000 - **6 weeks' rent** for properties with annual rent of £50,000 or more Any deposit taken above this cap must be returned. If the landlord refuses, the tenant can apply to the First-tier Tribunal. ## Admin Fees Specifically Charging an "admin fee" for organising contractors or processing deductions from the deposit is not a permitted payment. A landlord cannot deduct an admin fee from the deposit unless: - It relates to a genuine loss incurred from early termination requested by the tenant - It is a default fee explicitly permitted in the agreement and compliant with the Act An admin fee for "arranging contractors" is not permitted and is challengeable.
